content.problemStatement:
Let f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) be the sum of digits of the integer nnn when written in base BBB.

More formally, if n=∑i=0∞aiBin = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_i B^in=i=0∑∞​ai​Bi where aia_iai​ is an integer in the range [0,B−1][0, B-1][0,B−1], then f(n,B)=∑i=0∞aif(n, B) = \sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_if(n,B)=i=0∑∞​ai​.

Given QQQ queries, each consisting of three integers n,ln, ln,l and rrr. Find the value of BBB corresponding to which f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) is minimum for all l≤B≤rl \leq B \leq rl≤B≤r. If there are multiple such values, you can print any of them.


Input Format

The first line contains in single integer QQQ, the number of queries
Each of the next Q lines contain three space separated integers n,ln, ln,l and rrr respectively.


Output Format

For each query (`n l r`), print the value of base BBB which lies within [l,r][l, r][l,r] such that f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B) is minimum.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer Q (1 ≤ Q ≤ 10^3), the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains three space‑separated integers n, l and r (2 ≤ n ≤ 10^9, 2 ≤ l ≤ r ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single integer B (l ≤ B ≤ r) such that the digit sum f(n, B) is minimum among all bases in the interval. If several bases give the same minimum, output any of them. Print each answer on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
To compute f(n,B)f(n, B)f(n,B), first write the integer nnn in the form a0+a1B+a2B2+…a_0 + a_1 B + a_2 B^2 + \dots a0​+a1​B+a2​B2+… such that 0≤ai<B0 \leq a_i < B0≤ai​<B and aia_iai​ is an integer. It can be shown that such a representation is unique. The sum of the digits is then ∑i=0∞ai\sum\limits_{i=0}^{\infty} a_ii=0∑∞​ai​ (to be computed in base 10).
For example to compute f(216,2)f(216, 2)f(216,2), observe that 216=27+26+24+23216 = 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^4 + 2^3216=27+26+24+23, and so the sum of digits is 1+1+1+1=41 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 41+1+1+1=4.
Similarly, to compute  f(216,100)f(216, 100)f(216,100), write 216216216 as 2∗100+162 * 100 + 162∗100+16 and so the sum of digits is 2+16=182 + 16 = 182+16=18.
Test case 111: We have f(216,2)=f(216,3)=4f(216, 2) = f(216, 3) = 4f(216,2)=f(216,3)=4, f(216,4)=6f(216, 4) = 6f(216,4)=6, f(216,5)=8f(216, 5) = 8f(216,5)=8, f(216,6)=1f(216, 6) = 1f(216,6)=1 and finally f(216,7)=12f(216, 7) = 12f(216,7)=12. Clearly the minimum is obtained when B=6B = 6B=6.
Test case 222: Note that f(256,2)=f(256,4)f(256, 2) = f(256, 4)f(256,2)=f(256,4) = 111, therefore both the answers 222 and 444 will be considered correct.
Test case 333: f(31,3)=f(31,5)=3f(31, 3) = f(31, 5) = 3f(31,3)=f(31,5)=3 and f(31,4)=7f(31, 4) = 7f(31,4)=7, therefore both the answers 333 and 555 will be considered correct.

solution.approach:
For a fixed n the digit sum in base B is f(n,B)=∑ digits of n in base B. If l ≤ n ≤ r, choosing B=n gives representation "10" and f=1, which is optimal. Otherwise we need to search the interval [l,r].

Observation 1: For B > √n the representation has at most two digits: n = q·B + r (0 ≤ r < B) with q = ⌊n/B⌋. Then f = q + r = q + n - q·B. For a fixed q the sum decreases when B increases, therefore the best B for this q inside the interval is the largest B belonging to the interval, i.e. B = ⌊n/q⌋ (if it lies in [l,r]).

Observation 2: For B ≤ √n the number of digits can be larger, but √n ≤ 31623 for the given limits, so we can simply evaluate every B in this range directly.

Algorithm per query:
1. If l ≤ n ≤ r → answer = n.
2. Initialise bestB = l, bestSum = f(n,l).
3. For every B from l to min(r, ⌊√n⌋) compute f by repeated division and update the best pair.
4. For every possible quotient q from 1 to ⌊√n⌋:
   • B = n / q (integer division). If B ∈ [l,r] compute sum = q + n - q·B (same as digit sum) and update.
5. Finally output bestB.
The loop runs O(√n) ≤ 31623 steps per query, well within limits.

Correctness proof sketch:
- If n is inside the interval, B=n yields f=1, the global minimum because digit sums are positive integers.
- For B > √n the representation is exactly two digits, so f = q + r. For a fixed q the function of B is linear decreasing, thus the minimum over any sub‑interval occurs at its rightmost point. The rightmost point of the whole interval for this q is B = ⌊n/q⌋, which we examine. Any other B > √n either belongs to some q already considered or is larger than the rightmost point of its q‑interval, giving a larger sum.
- For B ≤ √n we enumerate all possibilities, guaranteeing the optimum among small bases.
- Consequently the algorithm checks every base that can be optimal, and returns a base with minimal digit sum.

Complexities: Time O(√n) per query, at most about 3·10^7 operations for the maximal test set. Space O(1).
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q·√n_max)  (≈ 3·10^7 operations in the worst case)
